message:
fix(telemetry/disk): classify whole disks vs partitions across all Linux naming schemes

/proc/diskstats partition-vs-whole-disk filtering used the heuristic
"ends in a digit AND doesn't start with nvme/loop". That mis-handled
two Linux naming families:

- mmc/SD: `mmcblk0` (whole card) was silently dropped because it
  ends in a digit. On any machine with only mmc storage, disk
  telemetry reported zero devices.
- nvme: `nvme0n1p1` (a partition) was kept because the prefix
  exception covered all of nvme, so I/O was double-counted against
  `nvme0n1`.

Replace the heuristic with an explicit is_partition_name() that
covers the three on-disk naming conventions:

- traditional (`sda`, `hda`, `vda`, `xvd`): whole disk is alpha-only,
  partitions append digits (`sda1`).
- nvme: whole namespace is `nvme<ctrl>n<ns>`; partitions add
  `p<digits>` (`nvme0n1p1`) — require the char before the 'p' to be
  a digit so we don't misclassify a hypothetical `nvmeXp` vendor
  name.
- mmc/SD: whole disk is `mmcblk<n>`; partitions append `p<digits>`
  (`mmcblk0p1`) — same `p<digits>` contract.

Whole-disk names that end in digits but aren't partitions
(`md0`, `ram7`, `sr0`, `fd0`, `dm-3`) are allow-listed via
WHOLE_DISK_PREFIX_EXACT.

Loop devices are still dropped up front (virtual-only).

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
